query: muscle action on mandible after fracture genioglossus

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications include the delayed union of the fractured mandible. If the fracture of the body of the mandible is bilateral along with the parasymphyseal, or condylar fractures, the airway may get impaired. This impairment is due to the muscular action that pulls the distal mandibular segment backward, resulting in obstruction of the oropharynx by the tongue. There may also be nerve damage such as neuropraxia, in which function takes around 4 to 6 weeks to return, or neurotmesis, in which function may take around 18 months to return.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: These forces that render the fracture unfavorable are exerted by various muscles such as masseter, temporalis, and medial pterygoid muscle. These muscles distract the proximal bony segment in the superomedial direction. Moreover, two additional muscles (mylohyoid and anterior belly of digastrics) may also play a role in displacing the segments in the posterior and inferior direction.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Genioglossus Muscle -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Myofunctional therapy is essential for restoring the function of the genioglossus muscle after surgery, trauma, or nerve injury. The literature shows that cervical trauma (whiplash) can negatively influence the function of the tongue, and osteopathic treatment can restore the lingual function.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mandible fractures can be classified by favorableness, based on the association between the direction of the fracture line and the way muscle action either reduces or distracts the fracture fragments. Mandibular fractures are favorable when muscles tend to draw the fracture fragments together and unfavorable when muscle forces displace fracture fragments. An example of a favorable fracture is an obliquely-oriented fracture just anterior to the angle, with the superior aspect of the fracture line situated posterior to the inferior aspect; this configuration causes the masseter to pull the fragments together and stabilize the fracture, meaning that surgical reduction may not be required. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Introduction -- Muscular Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The masseter muscle originates on the inferior aspect of the zygoma and zygomatic arch and inserts on the angle of the mandible. This is a powerful muscle of mastication, and its contraction can displace unstable bone segments inferiorly in certain cases.

retrieved_docs.title: Zygomatic Arch Fracture -- Introduction -- Muscular Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The zygomaticus major and minor are muscles of facial expression that originate on the zygoma and are inserted near the modiolus of the oral commissure to assist with corner-of-mouth elevation and lateralization during smiling.

retrieved_docs.title: Motor Evoked Potential -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle contraction during motor evoked potentials may cause inadvertent movement and positioning-related injuries. Bite injuries of the lips or tongue occur in 0.2% of cases and constitute the most frequent MEP-related complication. Mandibular fracture has been reported, as have cases of damage to the endotracheal tube caused by biting during MEP stimulation. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular body fractures usually occur between the distal aspect of the canine and a hypothetical line that corresponds to the region of anterior attachment of the masseter muscle. They may be classified based on the anatomic location, the direction of the fracture line, position of teeth relative to the fracture, and favorableness. [4] [5] Based on the fracture line direction and the effect of muscle distraction on the fracture fragments, the body fractures can classify into two types (favorable and unfavorable). In favorable fractures, the bony fragments are drawn together by the muscle distraction, whereas in unfavorable fractures, the bony segments become displaced by the muscle forces.

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Bilateral mandibular fractures are not uncommon, but edentulous patients make for a unique challenge. While there is no concern for malocclusion in edentulous patients, the lack of teeth mean that MMF cannot be used to stabilize fracture segments. In these cases, Gunning splints or external fixators may be used. Bilateral fractures of the mandibular body or ramus can also result in retraction of the anterior fracture segment, due to the action of the mylohyoid muscle, resulting in airway compromise and a potential need for emergent intubation or a surgical airway. Another special situation is the pediatric mandible fracture; most of these cases can be treated conservatively with a soft diet. Fortunately, it is very rare that children require MMF. Occasionally, ORIF is required, and many surgeons prefer to use resorbable plates and screws rather than titanium in order to minimize the potential for growth anomalies or injury to tooth buds. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Facial Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Mandible Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Fractures most likely requiring surgical reconstruction include those causing condyle displacement into the middle cranial fossa, limiting mandibular movement, bilateral fractures reducing mandibular height and causing an open bite, mobile anterior segments getting displaced by masticatory muscle action, and mobile alveolar ridge fractures needing wiring or braces. Orthodontics may be necessary if malocclusion persists after healing, regardless of fracture location.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular movement can influence forces surrounding mandibular fractures. The suprahyoid musculature and bite force puts downward anterior pressure on the mandible. The masseter, temporalis, and pterygoid muscles pull the posterior mandible upward. These opposing forces can reduce some (favorable) fractures and displace other (unfavorable) fractures. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Levator Anguli Oris Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The modiolus is a fibromuscular structure in the oral commissure lateral to the orbicularis oris at the corner of the mouth. The LAO's fibers travel anteroinferiorly and insert into the modiolus. The LAO is located in the deepest mimetic muscle layer along with the buccinator and mentalis, originating roughly 1 cm inferior to the infraorbital foramen within the maxilla's canine fossa. The LAO muscle fibers form an angle of approximately 37° with those of the more lateral zygomaticus major muscle. Other muscles attaching to the modiolus are the zygomaticus major, risorius, buccinator, depressor anguli oris, and orbicularis oris. These muscles pull the oral commissure in different directions, permitting facial expression variations. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Dislocation -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of botulinum toxin injection include hemorrhage, intravascular injection, diffusion into nearby tissue, dysphagia, toxin-induced velopharyngeal insufficiency. Botulinum toxin should be avoided in patients with an allergy to botulinum toxin and myasthenia gravis. [1] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
retrieved_docs.content: Various late effects of bone injuries are directly linked to the underlying fracture. The “Late effect of fracture of multiple bones,” the “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones), the Arabic phrases “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية” (late effects of fracture of the lower limbs), “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية” (late effects of fracture of the upper limbs), and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق فخذ” (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are all associated with “كسر,” the Arabic term for fracture. This relationship underscores that regardless of the anatomical region—whether multiple bones, the skull and facial skeleton, the upper or lower extremities, or the femoral neck—post‑traumatic sequelae are fundamentally connected to the original fracture event, highlighting the importance of monitoring and managing these late complications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network unifies head and neck trauma, intracranial and skull‑base injuries, all skeletal fractures, their associated tissue damage, poisoning mechanisms, external causes (MVT, aircraft crash), procedural treatments, and the spectrum of late‑effect sequelae under the central concepts of fracture and late effects of unspecified injury, providing a comprehensive framework for injury classification, management, and long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2396
retrieved_docs.content: The late effects of a fracture are closely linked to the original injury across multiple anatomical sites. The “Late effect of fracture of spine AND/OR trunk without spinal cord lesion” is associated with a fracture (كسر), indicating that patients may develop delayed complications even when the spinal cord is spared. Similarly, the “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of skull and facial bone fractures) are associated with a fracture (كسر), underscoring the potential for prolonged sequelae in craniofacial injuries. In Arabic, the phrases “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية” (late effects of lower‑extremity fractures), “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية” (late effects of upper‑extremity fractures), and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق عظم الفخذ” (late effects of femoral‑neck fractures) are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). Collectively, these relationships emphasize that delayed or late‑onset manifestations are a common aspect of fracture healing and management, regardless of whether the injury involves the spine, trunk, skull, facial bones, upper or lower limbs, or the femoral neck. Recognizing these late effects is essential for comprehensive patient care and for monitoring potential complications after the acute phase of a fracture.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1207

These entities represent various post‑acute sequelae that can follow different types of bone injuries and underscore the need for continued monitoring and management after the initial fracture has healed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2107

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 310
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late effect of fracture of the spine and/or trunk without spinal cord lesion, the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية (late effects of fracture of the lower extremities), the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية (late effects of fracture of the upper extremities), and the الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق عظم الفخذ (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are all described as being associated with كسر (fracture). This indicates that delayed complications can arise after fractures affecting various skeletal regions, including multiple bones, the spine or trunk, the lower and upper limbs, and the femoral neck, and that these sequelae are intrinsically linked to the initial fracture event.
